---
title: "Mean effects and heterogeneous heterogeneity"
output: rmarkdown::html_vignette
vignette: >
  %\VignetteIndexEntry{Mean effects and heterogeneous heterogeneity}
  %\VignetteEngine{knitr::rmarkdown}
  %\VignetteEncoding{UTF-8}
---

```{r, include = FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(collapse = TRUE, comment = "#>")
```

Meta-analysis in `drmTMB` is ordinary Gaussian distributional regression with
known sampling covariance supplied in the formula. It is not a separate family.

The implemented meta-analysis path accepts a vector of known sampling variances,
a diagonal matrix, a dense block-diagonal matrix, or a dense full covariance
matrix:

```r
drmTMB(
  bf(
    yi ~ x1 + meta_known_V(V = V),
    sigma ~ x1
  ),
  family = gaussian(),
  data = dat
)
```

Here `V` may be a vector/data column of known sampling variances or a known
sampling covariance matrix. If users have standard errors, they should square
them before fitting. For diagonal `V`, the likelihood is:

```text
yi_i ~ Normal(mu_i, vi_i + sigma_i^2)
mu_i = X_mu beta_mu
log(sigma_i) = X_sigma beta_sigma
```

In these equations, `Normal(a, b)` uses variance as the second argument.

For full `V`, the same model is written:

```text
y ~ MVN(mu, V + diag(sigma_i^2))
```

The `sigma` formula models extra heterogeneity after accounting for known
sampling error. In meta-analysis papers this is often written as `tau`, but the
package API keeps `sigma` for consistency with other Gaussian distributional
models.

A concise runnable example:

```r
dat <- data.frame(
  yi = c(0.10, 0.35, -0.05, 0.42),
  V = c(0.04, 0.03, 0.05, 0.02),
  x1 = c(0, 1, 0, 1)
)

fit <- drmTMB(
  bf(yi ~ x1 + meta_known_V(V = V), sigma ~ x1),
  family = gaussian(),
  data = dat
)
```

For planned bivariate meta-analysis, the known sampling covariance should
include the within-study covariance between the two effect-size estimates when
it is available. `meta_vcov_bivariate()` builds the common row-paired matrix:

```r
V <- meta_vcov_bivariate(
  v1 = c(0.04, 0.03),
  v2 = c(0.05, 0.02),
  cor12 = c(0.4, 0.2)
)
```

The stacking is `[y1_1, y2_1, y1_2, y2_2, ...]`. This helper is implemented
now; bivariate fitting with known sampling covariance is the next likelihood
task, where the known sampling covariance will be added to the fitted residual
or between-study covariance.

## Implementation caveats

- Dense full covariance is useful for correlated effect sizes and can also
  represent block-diagonal covariance. Sparse covariance storage is planned for
  larger phylogenetic and spatial workloads.
- Negative and missing known variances are rejected or removed through the same
  row-retention rules as the model data; full matrices must be symmetric and
  positive semidefinite after row subsetting.
- The API should keep `family = gaussian()` and `sigma ~ ...`; it should not
  introduce `meta_gaussian()` or `tau ~` syntax without a separate design
  decision.
